title: Deep Memory of iOS
description: In-depth analysis of iOS memory: From Memory Graph to OOM (Out of Memory) location. Detailed explanation of Dirty/Clean Memory, Compressed Memory mechanisms, and memory optimization strategies.
content:
Memory Footprint

graph TD
  subgraph Typical_app_memory_profile
    Dirty_memory
    Clean_memory
    Compressed
  end


graph LR

  subgraph Memory_warnings
    App_is_not_always_the_cause
    Compressor_complicates_freeing_memory
    Prefer_policy_changes_over_cache_purging
  end

  Memory_warnings -.-> Pages

  subgraph Pages
    Page_types --> Dirty_memory
    Page_types --> Clean_memory
    Typically_16KB
  end
  
  subgraph Memory_mapped_files
    File-backed_memory
    Read-only_files_are_clean
    Kernel_manages_when_file_is_in_RAM
  end

  Memory_mapped_files --> files

  subgraph files
    image.jpeg
    Blob.data
    Training.model
  end
  

  Clean_memory --> Data_that_can_be_paged_out_of_memory
  Data_that_can_be_paged_out_of_memory --> Memory_mapped_files
  Data_that_can_be_paged_out_of_memory --> __DATA_CONST

  subgraph Frameworks
  __DATA_CONST
  __DATA
  __DATA_DIRTY
  end

  Dirty_memory --> Memory_written_by_an_app
  Memory_written_by_an_app --> All_heap_allocations
  Memory_written_by_an_app --> Decoded_image_buffers
  Memory_written_by_an_app --> __DATA
  Memory_written_by_an_app --> __DATA_DIRTY

  All_heap_allocations --> malloc
  All_heap_allocations --> Allay
  All_heap_allocations --> NSCache
  All_heap_allocations --> UIViews
  All_heap_allocations --> String

  Decoded_image_buffers --> CGRasterData
  Decoded_image_buffers --> ImageIO
  

  subgraph Memory_compressor
    No_traditional_disk_swap
    Compresses_unaccessed_pages
    Decompresses_pages_upon_access
  end

  Memory_compressor --> Pages

Pages

It consists of multiple pages, each occupying 16KB. If that's not enough, more will be requested.

Memory is use = Number of Pages X Page size

Clean & dirty pages

Clean Memory
Memory-mapped files: These are files that are loaded from the disk into memory but not written to or modified by the application, such as images, data blocks, and training models.
Read-only data: This includes constant data or read-only global variables in the application, such as strings, constant arrays, etc.

In short, data that can be paged out of memory can be paginated out of memory by the operating system to reclaim memory space. Paged data is typically stored in other areas of the disk, such as a page file or swap partition (which does not exist in iOS), so it can be reloaded into memory when needed.

Dirty Memory
Dynamically allocated objects: Objects created dynamically using methods such as malloc and calloc, such as dynamically allocated strings and arrays.
Modifying object properties: This involves modifying or updating already created objects, including assigning values ​​to properties and modifying array elements.
Image buffer decoding: Decodes the image, converting the compressed format into raw pixel data in memory.
Cached data: A caching mechanism implemented by the application itself to store frequently accessed data, such as the results of network requests and calculations.

In short, the memory written by an app mainly comes from the data created by the app.

Frameworks

A good practice is to use the singletons and the global initializers of the frameworks to alleviate the amount of dirty memory generated by their usage.

Using the framework's singleton pattern and global initializer is a good practice that can reduce the amount of dirty memory generated during their use.
The Singleton pattern is a design pattern that ensures that a class has only one instance and provides a single global access point. When objects in a framework are created using the Singleton pattern, they persist in memory, reducing frequent memory allocation and deallocation operations and thus minimizing the generation of dirty memory.
A global initializer is an initializer that runs when the framework is linked or a class is loaded. By performing necessary operations and initialization steps in the global initializer, dirty memory generated during framework use can be reduced.

In short, to achieve this effect, it's similar to defining static variables as const (const is determined at compile time) in a design paradigm where they are immutable.

If only a singleton is designed, and the properties and methods inside are dynamically created and modified, it will ultimately end up in dirty memory.

Compressed

This capability was introduced in iOS 7.

In operating systems, memory is a finite resource. To maximize the use of available memory, memory compression technology has been introduced. A memory compressor monitors the system's memory usage and compresses pages in memory as needed. Unused pages typically occupy a significant amount of memory; compressing these unused pages reduces memory usage.

When an application needs to access a compressed page, the memory compressor decompresses the page before accessing it. This process is transparent; the application is unaware that the page is compressed. By decompressing on access, the memory compressor ensures that the application can correctly access the required data.

Memory compression techniques are particularly effective for systems or applications with a large number of unused pages. They can reduce memory footprint, extend the lifespan of available memory, and improve overall system performance. However, it's important to note that the memory compression and decompression processes increase CPU load and may cause slight access delays.

In short, compressed memory may suddenly increase during use, and CPU usage may also surge.

Memory warnings

What should you do when your app is running out of memory? And why does this memory shortage sometimes occur?
